coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-based stimuli-responsive hydrogel for in-situ detection of thiram on fruits and vegetables

coredata.dc:creator: Li, Hongxia
Hu, Yanan
Lin, Zhen
Yan, Xu
Sun, Chunyan
Yao, Dong
coredata.dc:description: Stimuli-responsive hydrogel possesses a strong loading capacity to embed luminescent indicators for constructing food safety sensors, which are suitable for field application. In this work, a fluorescent hydrogel sensor was fabricated by incorporating Ag+-modified carbon dots (CDs-Ag+) into a sodium alginate (SA) hydrogel for in-situ detection of thiram. The fluorescence of CDs was quenched due to the combined effects of electrostatic adsorption and electron transfer between Ag+ and CDs. The formation of an AgS bond between thiram and Ag+ facilitates the release of CDs, causing subsequently fluorescence recovery. Combined with smartphone and analysis software, the fluorescence color change of the hydrogel sensor was converted into data information for quantitative detection of thiram. Such a sample-to-result step is completed within 10 min. Notably, the in-situ detection experiment of thiram in fruit and vegetable samples confirmed the practical application of the hydrogel sensor. Therefore, the hydrogel sensor provides a new research direction for the in-situ detection of pesticide residues in the monitoring of food safety.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pesticide detection
Carbon dots
Hydrogel
Sensor
In-situ detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of thiram by CDs-Ag+-based SA hydrogel
Characterization of CDs and the fluorescence quenching mechanism of CDs-Ag+
Mechanism and performance of sensing thiram
Construction and application of CDs-Ag+-based SA hydrogel

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to acknowledge the financial support by the National Key R&D Program of China (No. 2023YFB3208301), National Natural Science Foundation of China (62371204 and 62171194), Natural Science Foundation of Jilin Province (20240101259JC), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central University (2024-JCXK-21), the On-budge Capital Construction Fund of Jilin Province Development and Reform Commission (2023C014), the Jilin University Targeted Assistance Research Funds for Rural Revitalization Project (2023-2024DDBF-0013), the Jilin Provincial Education Department Science and Technology Project (JJKH20241251KJ).

The authors would like to acknowledge the financial support by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 62171194 and 62371204 ), Natural Science Foundation of Jilin Province ( 20230402023GH ), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central University ( 2024-JCXK-21 ), the On-budge Capital Construction Fund of Jilin Province Development and Reform Commission ( 2023C014 ), the Jilin University Targeted Assistance Research Funds for Rural Revitalization Project ( 2023-2024DDBF-0013 ), Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing ( CSTB2022NSCQ-MSX1102 and cstc2021j-cyj-msxmX0927 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of CDs. (A) TEM images of the CDs. Inset is the HRTEM image of the CDs. (B) Size distribution diagram of the CDs. (C) AFM image of the CDs and the inset shows the height profile analysis. (D) UV–vis spectra and FL emission spectra of CDs with inserted photographs of CDs dissolved in water under UV light. (E) Raman and (F) FT-IR spectra of the CDs. High-resolution XPS (G) C 1 s, (H) O 1 s, and (I) N 1 s spectra of CDs.

(A) Effect of metal ions on the fluorescence of CDs. (B) Linear plot of CDs fluorescence quenching rate [(F0 F)/F0] versus Ag+ concentration. (F0 is the initial fluorescence of CDs, and F is the quenched fluorescence of CD.) (C) TEM images and EDS energy spectra of CDs-Ag+. (D) Zeta potential data for CDs and CDs-Ag+. (E) UV–Vis absorption of CDs before and after the addition of Ag+. (F) Fluorescence lifetimes of CDs in the presence of Ag+ (1.0 mM and 5.0 mM). Data in (A), (B) and (D) are shown as mean ± SD (n = 3).

(A) Schematic illustrations of thiram detection mechanism. (B) Fluorescence spectra of CDs in the presence of Ag+/thiram and Ag+. (C) Response degree of different concentrations of Ag+ to thiram. F1 is the CDs fluorescence recovered after adding thiram. (D) Fluorescence time course of the CDs-Ag+ system with or without thiram. (E) Fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs-Ag+ system in the presence of different concentrations of thiram from 1.0 ng mL−1 to 7500 ng mL−1. (F) Relationship between fluorescence intensity and Log [thiram]. Data in (F) are shown as mean ± SD (n = 3).

(A) Schematic illustrations of the CDs-Ag+-based SA hydrogel system. (B) The formation of SA hydrogel. (C) and (D) SEM images of the CDs-Ag+-based SA hydrogel. (E) R-channel analysis of Ag+ and CDs concentration optimization in SA hydrogels. (F) True-color images are split into RGB channels, and the (R/B) value response is calculated for obtaining pseudo color. (G) The relationship of the (R/B) value response with thiram concentration. Data in (G) are shown as mean ± SD (n = 3).

Selectivity (A) and anti-interference capacity (B) of the CDs-Ag+-based SA hydrogel to thiram. (C) Flowchart of the CDs-Ag+-based SA hydrogel for detection of fruit and vegetable samples. (D) The CDs-Ag+-based SA hydrogel assay for actual samples (grape, strawberry, Chinese cabbage, and spinach). Data in (A) and (B) are shown as mean ± SD (n = 3).
Schematic diagram of the CDs-Ag+-based SA hydrogel for detecting thiram residues in fruits and vegetables.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-based stimuli-responsive hydrogel for in-situ detection of thiram on fruits and vegetables

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food Quality and Safety, College of Food Science and Engineering, Jilin University, Changchun 130062, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Food Quality and Safety, College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Food Quality and Safety, College of Food Science and Engineering, Jilin University, Changchun 130062, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Electronic Science and Engineering, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Electronic Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Electronic Science and Engineering, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, College of Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Supramolecular Structure and Materials, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Stimuli-responsive hydrogel possesses a strong loading capacity to embed luminescent indicators for constructing food safety sensors, which are suitable for field application. In this work, a fluorescent hydrogel sensor was fabricated by incorporating Ag+-modified carbon dots (CDs-Ag+) into a sodium alginate (SA) hydrogel for in-situ detection of thiram. The fluorescence of CDs was quenched due to the combined effects of electrostatic adsorption and electron transfer between Ag+ and CDs. The formation of an AgS bond between thiram and Ag+ facilitates the release of CDs, causing subsequently fluorescence recovery. Combined with smartphone and analysis software, the fluorescence color change of the hydrogel sensor was converted into data information for quantitative detection of thiram. Such a sample-to-result step is completed within 10 min. Notably, the in-situ detection experiment of thiram in fruit and vegetable samples confirmed the practical application of the hydrogel sensor. Therefore, the hydrogel sensor provides a new research direction for the in-situ detection of pesticide residues in the monitoring of food safety.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In modern agriculture, pesticides are essential since they efficiently reduce or eliminate fungus, weeds, and pests to boost crop yields (Su, Li, Yan, Lin, & Lu, 2021). Thiram, a typical organosulfur fungicide, is frequently used to protect fruits and vegetables from fungal infection (Queffelec et al., 2001). However, thiram has leaked into the environment due to its abuse and improper disposal, posing a new severe threat to ecosystems and food safety (A. Li et al., 2022). Furthermore, thiram is mildly toxic and irritates the skin and mucous membranes, which causes a health threat (H. Li, Li, Wu, Yang, & Li, 2021). Especially, human long-term exposure to excessive thiram can inactivate transcription factor NF-kB and hypoxia-inducible factor, leading to reproductive and nervous system dysfunction (Pu, Huang, Xu, & Sun, 2021; Q. Yang, Li, Li, & Li, 2021). Therefore, the rapid and sensitive detection of thiram is essential to assess food safety.

Currently, several methods, including gas chromatography, liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry, surface-enhanced Raman scattering, and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay, can accurately and effectively detect pesticide residues (Belarbi et al., 2021; Piao et al., 2009; Steinborn et al., 2016; S. Wang, Hao, Zhao, & Chen, 2023). However, the instrument operating and preparation processes are sophisticated, time-consuming, and expensive (Su et al., 2021). Fluorescence sensing technology has rapidly developed in the field of rapid detection due to its quick and sensitive recognition of analytes (Luo et al., 2020; Oh et al., 2020). Carbon dots (CDs) are a new class of photoluminescent nanomaterials, which have the advantages of low toxicity, light bleaching resistance, and convenient synthesis, providing a new idea for the establishment of a fluorescence sensing platform (B. Bin Chen et al., 2023; L. Wang et al., 2020; Xia, Zhu, Feng, Yang, & Yang, 2019). For example, a proportional fluorescence sensing system was developed to detect thiram by leveraging the synergistic effect of CDs and copper nanoclusters (L. Chen et al., 2022). Utilizing the internal filtration effect between gold nanoparticles and CDs, a “on-off” fluorescence nano-sensor was designed for thiram detection (L. Fu et al., 2022). However, most CDs-based strategies need sample pretreatment and time-costing (30–80 min), which limits their application in field rapid testing (Lu et al., 2022; Wang, Sun, Pan, & Wang, 2022). Therefore, a direct, rapid, and reliable sensing platform is urgently required to detect thiram in fruits and vegetables.

Hydrogels are a class of highly hydrated polymers with a three-dimensional network structure, drawing increasing attention in fabricating portable sensors (Chae et al., 2023). Sodium alginate (SA) is a type of natural linear polysaccharide formed by the copolymerization of β-D-mannuronic acid and α-L-guluronic acid, which possesses non-toxic and harmless with good biocompatibility (W. Wang, Qu, Zhang, Teng, & Huang, 2021). The cross-linking process of SA is realized by ion exchange with mild reaction conditions, short time, and simple operation (Lu et al., 2023). Researchers have developed a series of fluorescent hydrogel enzyme-based sensors to detect pesticides. For example, the SA hydrogel was loaded with AuNCs@ZIF and acetylcholinesterase to construct a portable sensor for chlorpyrifos detection (Yan et al., 2022). Although enzyme-based hydrogel sensors exhibit high sensitivity and rapid response characteristics, some challenges limit their practical application, e.g. (i) Enzymes activity is easily affected by the surrounding environment (e.g. pH and temperature) (Zhao et al., 2018). (ii) The complexity of the sample matrix and the complicated extraction steps also pose challenges to accurately detecting pesticides (L. Zhang, Tian, Bachman, Zhang, & Huang, 2020). In-situ detection technology detects pesticides by carrying fluorescent nanomaterials on paper or tape substrates and directly wiping or sticking them on the surface of samples. It offers advantages such as fast equilibrium rates, low matrix interference, and addresses challenges posed by biological enzymes (D. Chen et al., 2021; Watthaisong et al., 2022; D. Zhang, Pu, Huang, & Sun, 2021). The combination of in-situ detection technology and fluorescent hydrogel can realize nondestructive detection of pesticides, which provides an effective means to improve detection efficiency and reduce costs.

In this study, we successfully constructed a CDs-Ag+-based SA hydrogel with stimuli-responsive characteristics and adjustable fluorescence behavior (Scheme 1
). CDs were designed via solvothermal controllable polymerization of L-glutamic acid and o-phenylenediamine with stable yellow emission (λ = 570 nm). Ag+ binds to CDs through electrostatic adsorption, causing a decrease in fluorescence intensity due to electron transfer. After the introduction of thiram, a stable AgS bond was formed between thiram and Ag+ to induce the release of CDs, which led to the recovery of fluorescence. Therefore, the CDs-Ag+-based SA hydrogel exhibits stimuli-responsive characteristics to thiram. The CDs-Ag+-based SA hydrogel can be directly applied to the surface of fruits and vegetables by its viscidity, enabling rapid in-situ detection of thiram. We utilize smartphones for imaging and analyzing the test results. The CDs-Ag+-based SA hydrogel effectively avoids sample pretreatment and directly detects thiram within 10 min, which meets the growing demand for practical analysis and provides a new idea for realizing rapid food safety detection.

L-glutamic acid (99%) and o-phenylenediamine (99%) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd., China. N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) was purchased from Tianjin Tiantai Fine Chemical Co., Ltd., China. silver nitrate (99%), potassium nitrate (99%), magnesium sulfate anhydrous (99%), cupric sulfate anhydrous (99%), ferrous sulfate (90%), Calcium chloride anhydrous (99%), sodium chloride (99.5%), sodium phosphate dibasic (99%), monosodium phosphate (99%) and sodium alginate (90%) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd., China. All chemicals were used without any further purification.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) images were obtained by using a JEOL JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope. The sample was placed on a carbon support film with a Cu grid. Atomic force microscope (AFM) images were obtained by Bruker Nanoscope. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were measured by Shimadzu 2600 UV–vis spectrophotometer. The fluorescence emission spectrum was measured by Hitachi F-7100 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was performed by Bruker VERTEX 80 V. Raman spectroscopy was measured by a Horiba LabRAM HR Evolution spectrometer with a 532 nm laser. Fluorescence (FL) spectra and FL lifetime were measured by Edinburgh FLS980. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out by a Scienta ESCA200 spectrometer with a Ka x-ray source. The hydrogel picture was taken by a hand-held ultraviolet light under the excitation of 365 nm.

The CDs were synthesized via a solvothermal procedure. Briefly, 0.11 g of L-glutamic acid and 0.16 g of o-phenylenediamine were dissolved in 25 mL of DMF. Then the mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave (100 mL) and heated at 210 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was filtered with a 0.22 μm membrane. The obtained solution was concentrated by a rotary evaporator, after which the dark product was dissolved in deionized water and centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 20 min to remove the aggregated particles. Then, dichloromethane was added to remove organic impurities. The purification process was repeated three times and the supernatant was freeze-dried to obtain the final CDs powders.

To make CDs-Ag+-based SA hydrogel, CDs (50 μL, 1.0 mg mL−1) and AgNO3 (50 μL, 5 mM) were mixed well with SA solution (100 μL, 20.0 mg mL−1) in a 96-well microtiter plate, and aqueous hydrogel were obtained by introducing 30 μL Ca2+ solution (5.0 mg mL−1) into the above solution. The obtained CDs-Ag+-based SA hydrogel can be stored at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of thiram by CDs-Ag+-based SA hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: For the detection of pesticides, different concentrations of thiram (50 μL) were added to the CDs-Ag+-based SA hydrogel and the reaction was carried out for 10 min. The CDs-Ag+-based SA hydrogel was irradiated with UV light and color images were captured using a smartphone camera and finally analyzed using ImageJ software.

Real samples including strawberries, grapes, Chinese cabbage, and spinach used in in-situ detection were purchased from local Supermarket through random sampling. All samples were thoroughly washed several times with ultrapure water and dried. Then, each fruit and vegetable are randomly divided into four groups, and one sample is randomly selected from each group as the sample to be tested. The contaminated samples were prepared by spraying thiram solutions of different concentrations (0, 200, 1000, and 2000 ng mL−1) onto the surfaces. Finally, the prepared SA hydrogel was attached to the surface of the sample to be tested through a syringe. After 10 min of reaction, it was irradiated by ultraviolet light, then captured by smartphone, and finally analyzed by ImageJ software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs and the fluorescence quenching mechanism of CDs-Ag+
ce:section.ce:para: Yellow-emissive CDs were facilely prepared through a one-step treatment of L-glutamic acid and o-phenylenediamine under solvothermal conditions in DMF. The morphology of CDs is scrutinized through transmission electron microscopy (TEM), revealing their well-dispersed spherical structure (Fig. 1A). The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (inset of Fig. 1A) exhibits distinct lattice fringes with a spacing distance of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphite (Wang et al., 2022). The average particle size is determined to be 2.7 nm (Fig. 1B). Additionally, atomic force microscopy (AFM) showcases monodisperse CDs with a height of approximately 2–3 nm (Fig. 1C), consistent with TEM findings. The UV–visible absorption and fluorescence (FL) spectra of aqueous CD solutions are presented in Fig. 1D. Three absorption peaks at 243, 268, and 384 nm are observed in the UV–vis spectrum, attributed to the π-π⁎ transition of the CC or CN bond and the n-π⁎ transition of the CO bond in the carbon core, respectively (Ding, Yu, Wei, & Xiong, 2016). The CDs exhibit vibrant yellow emission with a FL peak centered at 570 nm (inset of Fig. 1D). The Raman spectrum of CDs delineates four deconvoluted peaks at 1549, 1645, 1468, and 1240 cm−1, denoted as A, G, D, and D* bands (Fig. 1E). These are ascribed to amorphous carbon in sp. hybridization in carbon polycrystals, the stretching and breathing modes of sp2 domain edge defects, and the disordered graphite lattice, respectively (Ferrari & Robertson, 2001; Wu, Lin, Cong, Liu, & Tan, 2018). The calculated ratio of the integrated intensity of the D-band to the G-band is 0.84, indicative of a graphite-like structure (Y. Liu et al., 2022). The abundant functional groups on the CD surface are analyzed using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) (Fig. 1F). The broad absorption bands at 3700 cm−1-3000 cm−1 and 600 cm−1 - 800 cm−1 are assigned to the O-H/N-H stretching vibration and the NH in-plane bending vibration, respectively. Additionally, the sharp peak at 1679 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of C=O/C=N. Absorption bands in the range of 1470 cm−1-1360 cm−1 and 1330 cm−1 -1210 cm−1 are also observed, assignable to the stretching vibrations of C-N/C-O. Due to the presence of these functional groups, the CDs possess good solubility and stability in water. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) further unveils that CDs are composed of C, O, and N elements, consistent with FT-IR results (Fig. S1). The C1s XPS spectrum is deconvoluted into four binding energy peaks, including C=C/C-C (284.3 eV), CN (284.8 eV), CO (285.8 eV), and CO (287.7 eV), and the O1s XPS spectrum contains CO and CO peaks at 530.9 eV and 532.3 eV, respectively (Fig. 1G, H). The N1s XPS spectrum exhibits three peaks at 398.4 eV, 399.5 eV, and 400.7 eV, attributed to pyridine N, pyrrole N, and graphite N, respectively, indicating N atoms doping into the graphene center (Fig. 1I). The optical stability of CDs is also studied. After continuous irradiation for 120 min under ultraviolet light, the fluorescence intensity of CDs hardly shows attenuation, showing favorable photobleaching resistance (Fig. S2).

To construct stimuli-responsive CDs-Ag+-based SA hydrogel, different metal ions (Ag+, K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe2+, and Cu2+) were added to the CDs solution to study the quenching ability. As shown in Fig. 2A, the addition of Ag+ to CDs significantly reduces fluorescence intensity, whereas the addition of other metal ions has no significant effect on fluorescence intensity. The experimental results indicate that Ag+ efficiently quenches CDs. As expected, the fluorescence intensity of CDs gradually decreases with the increment of Ag+ concentration ranging from 1.0 mM to 7.0 mM. There is an excellent linear relationship between the concentration of Ag+ and the fluorescence quenching degree (F0 F)/F0 of CDs (Fig. 2B). Subsequently, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of CDs with Ag+ was explored. In Fig. 2C, the TEM image demonstrates that the presence of Ag+ leads to aggregation of CDs. EDS analysis reveals the distribution of Ag and N elements on these aggregated CDs, providing evidence for the adsorption of Ag+ onto the CDs. The Zeta potential further confirms the electrostatic adsorption and electron transfer between Ag+ and CDs. Upon adding Ag+, the Zeta potential of CDs experienced a change from −16.5 mV to +9.0 mV, indicating the transfer of electrons from CDs to Ag+ (Fig. 2D) (Y. Yang et al., 2017). No prominent new peaks are observed in the UV–Vis absorption spectra of Ag+, CDs, and CDs-Ag+, indicating the absence of a coordination compound forming between CDs and Ag+ (Liu et al., 2022). Furthermore, the absorption peak intensity of CDs within the 240–280 nm range is significantly enhanced upon the addition of Ag+. This enhancement can be attributed to the promotion of non-radiative recombination of excited electrons by Ag+ (Zhou, He, Ya, Zhong, & Deng, 2017). The disappearance of the absorption peak of CDs at 385 nm suggests that the electron transfer occurs between Ag+ and the CO bond (Fig. 2E) (Zong et al., 2014). We fitted the time-resolved concentration decay curves of CDs and CDs-Ag+ with the double exponential formula (Fig. 2F). The short life τ1 of CDs is 2.77 ns, while the long lifetime τ2 is 6.61 ns (Table S1). These lifetimes corresponded to the core state and surface state of CDs, respectively (Khan, Gupta, Verma, & Nandi, 2015). After adding Ag+, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs decreases obviously, indicating the occurrence of charge transfer between Ag+ and CDs (Ju & Chen, 2014). The fluorescence of CDs is quenched due to the combined effects of electrostatic adsorption and electron transfer between Ag+ and CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism and performance of sensing thiram
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the excellent optical properties of CDs and their selective reaction to Ag+, a stimuli-responsive CDs-Ag+-based SA hydrogel sensor for detecting thiram was constructed. CDs are evenly distributed throughout the solution and emit a steady yellow fluorescence. Ag+ binds to CDs through electrostatic adsorption, causing a decrease in fluorescence intensity due to electron transfer. Following the addition of thiram, the fluorescence of the CDs-Ag+ system is immediately restored. The combination of thiram and Ag+ forms a more stable AgS bond (Fu et al., 2020; Liu, Jin, Wu, & Liang, 2022), which leads to the release of CDs from the CDs-Ag+ system and subsequent recovery of fluorescence (Fig. 3A). Fig. 3B shows the authenticity and feasibility of this method. To create a more sensitive thiram detection method, we optimized the reaction conditions of the CDs-Ag+ system. Firstly, the concentration of Ag+ was optimized. The relationship between Ag+ and CDs has been discussed above, and it was found that the fluorescence intensity of CDs is linearly correlated with the concentration of Ag+ (Fig. 2B). Therefore, 1.0 μg mL−1 thiram is introduced into the system. The response of Ag+ to thiram is determined by observing the recovery degree of CDs fluorescence, and the optimal response concentration of Ag+ is selected. When the concentration of Ag+ was 5.0 mM, the fluorescence recovery degree of CDs reached the maximum, indicating that 5.0 mM Ag+ had the most significant response to thiram (Fig. 3C). Subsequently, the system's reaction time, pH, and temperature were optimized, respectively. Based on Fig. 3D, it was observed that the reactions among Ag+, CDs, and thiram exhibited a high degree of rapidity and stability. Additionally, the reaction phenomenon of the system was most pronounced when the reaction pH was adjusted to 7.5, and the reaction temperature was maintained at 25 °C (Fig. S3). Finally, the optimized experimental parameters were selected as follows: Ag+ concentration of 5.0 mM, reaction time of 10 min, pH of 7.5, and temperature of 25 °C. A study was conducted to investigate how different concentrations of thiram affect the fluorescence properties of the CDs-Ag+ system. With the increase of thiram concentration, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs-Ag+ system gradually recovered. When the concentration of thiram reaches a particular value, the quenched fluorescence even returns to the original value of CDs (Fig. 3E). The fitted curve between the fluorescence intensity and thiram concentration (1.0 to 7500 ng mL−1) corresponded to a linear equation: y = 91.79 Log [Thiram] + 119.06 (R2 = 0.996) (Fig. 3F). These results confirm that the CDs-Ag+ system can be utilized for detecting thiram through fluorescence recovery.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction and application of CDs-Ag+-based SA hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: CDs-Ag+ composites were immobilized into three-dimensional hydrogel nanostructures during the preparation of SA hydrogel. Stable fluorescent hydrogels were formed by introducing Ca2+ (Fig. 4A) (H. Li et al., 2022). Under UV light, well-formed SA hydrogels have no fluorescent background and maintain a fixed shape when the glass vial is tilted (Fig. 4B). After adding the CDs-Ag+ complex to the SA hydrogel, a light yellow fluorescence appears in the glass bottle. The yellow fluorescence deepens with the addition of thiram. The transmittance test and fluorescence intensity comparison of the SA hydrogel confirmed its good transparency and its non-interference with signal output (Fig. S4). To create CDs-Ag+-based SA hydrogels with optimal shape and viscosity for in-situ detection, the concentration of Ca2+ was optimized. Rheological test results demonstrated that 5 mg mL−1 Ca2+ provides the SA hydrogel with suitable viscosity (Fig. S5). Additionally, the SA hydrogel cross-linked with 5 mg mL−1 Ca2+ maintains its shape well and exhibits stable adhesion on inclined surfaces and in inverted centrifuge tubes (Fig. S6). The SEM image shows the three-dimensional layer structure of SA hydrogel (Fig. 4C), and the CDs-Ag+ complex is successfully loaded (Fig. 4D). With the successful loading of CDs-Ag+ complex, the specific surface area of SA hydrogel decreased, and the pore size remained almost unchanged (Fig. S7 and Table S2). To achieve optimal performance, the concentrations of CDs and Ag+ in SA hydrogel were optimized, and the heat map was drawn (Fig. 4E). The results demonstrate that Ag+ greatly influences the fluorescence of CDs. With the increment of Ag+ concentration, the color of the hydrogel embedded with 1.0 mg mL−1 CDs exhibits the most significant change (from green to purple). Moreover, when the Ag+ concentration reaches 5.0 mM, the system shows a gradient response to thiram (0 to 2000 ng mL−1). Thus, 5.0 mM Ag+ and 1.0 mg mL−1 CDs are selected to fabricate the hydrogel sensor. The fluorescence intensity of CDs-Ag+-based SA hydrogel does not decay after continuous ultraviolet light irradiation for 120 min, showing good photobleaching resistance (Fig. S8).

We successfully constructed a CDs-Ag+-based SA hydrogel platform for detecting thiram. The red channel signal of the CDs-Ag+-based SA hydrogel increases with the concentration of thiram, while the blue channel signal decreases, resulting in a dual-signal color change (Fig. 4F). The signal (R/B) is normalized by dividing the red intensity value and the blue intensity value to obtain pseudo colors (bottom panel of Fig. 4F), which enhances the accuracy and sensitivity of the corresponding color via amplifying the response signal. The R/B value increases nonlinearly with the concentration of thiram from 5.0 to 5000 ng mL−1 (R2 = 0.998) (Fig. 4G). The detection limit (LOD) of the R/B response is calculated to be 4.0 ng mL−1. This method exhibits better sensitivity and portability than previous strategies (Table S3).

Selectivity and anti-interference ability are the main characteristics used to evaluate the practicality of portable sensors. Common substances such as metal ions, proteins, and pesticides are selected to investigate the selectivity and anti-interference ability of the CDs-Ag+-based SA hydrogel. The results indicate that the fluorescence intensity of the CDs-Ag+-based SA hydrogel is significantly enhanced in the presence of thiram (200 ng mL−1), while other pesticides (200 ng mL−1) do not affect the fluorescence intensity of the CDs-Ag+-based SA hydrogel (Fig. 5A). Meanwhile, the presence of other substances (2000 ng mL−1) hardly affects the recognition of thiram by the CDs-Ag+-based SA hydrogel either (Fig. 5B). The above results confirm that the CDs-Ag+-based SA hydrogel specifically recognizes thiram and has good anti-interference ability.

To better apply the SA hydrogel sensor to the in-situ detection of thiram, an ordinary syringe is employed to store the CDs-Ag+-based SA hydrogel. When the SA hydrogel is applied to the surface of fruits and vegetables sprayed with thiram, a noticeable yellow fluorescence can be observed under UV light after a 10-min incubation (Fig. 5C). To verify the practicability of SA hydrogel for detection, environmental water (Songhua River water and paddy field water) and milk were also employed as test samples. Different concentrations of thiram (0, 100, 200, 1000 ng mL−1) were added to test samples using the standard addition method for recovery testing. The recovery rate of thiram ranged between 85.84% and 117.23%, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of less than 7.61%, which is consistent with that of high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Table S4). Next, the SA hydrogel was applied to different vegetables (spinach and Chinese cabbage) and fruits (grapes and strawberries) for in-situ detection. Thiram was sprayed on the samples with various concentrations (200, 1000, and 2000 ng mL−1) for testing. Then, the prepared CDs-Ag+-based SA hydrogel was directly attached to the surface of the samples (Fig. 5D). The SA hydrogel prepared in this study exhibited excellent harmonious conformity not only to samples with smooth surfaces (grapes) but also to those with rough surfaces (strawberries, Chinese cabbage). Because the color background of different samples has different interference with the color of the hydrogel, the experimental results (F/F0) are normalized, and the detection results of the four samples are relatively consistent. In addition, HPLC was utilized to confirm the detection reliability of the CDs-Ag+-based SA hydrogel on Chinese cabbage (Fig. S9 and Table S5). The maximum residue limit of thiram in fruits and vegetables is 5.0 μg mL−1 (GB 2763–2021), meaning that the CDs-Ag+-based SA hydrogel meets the test requirements. Thus, the combination of CDs-Ag+-based SA hydrogel and ImageJ software has excellent utility and reliability in the on-site detection of thiram residues within 10 min.

In summary, a CDs-Ag+-based SA hydrogel for in-situ detection of thiram is developed. The SA hydrogel with favorable sensitivity, stability and reliability can accurately detect thiram residues in fruits and vegetables. The CDs-Ag+-based SA hydrogel has the following advantages: short reaction time and in-situ detection. Based on the fluorescence signal change of CDs-Ag+, the SA hydrogel has a better response to thiram and achieves a sensitive detection of 4.0 ng mL−1. Meanwhile, the SA hydrogel has excellent anti-interference ability and selectivity. Using ImageJ software to assist data analysis, the fluorescent color image of hydrogel was converted into digital information for field quantitative analysis of thiram. The CDs-Ag+-based SA hydrogel successfully detects thiram residues in fruits and vegetables without complicated processing steps. Such a SA hydrogel contributes to the development of pesticide detection for constructing intelligent agriculture.

Hongxia Li: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Zhen Lin: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Xu Yan: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Data curation. Chunyan Sun: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Dong Yao: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the financial support by the National Key R&D Program of China (No. 2023YFB3208301), National Natural Science Foundation of China (62371204 and 62171194), Natural Science Foundation of Jilin Province (20240101259JC), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central University (2024-JCXK-21), the On-budge Capital Construction Fund of Jilin Province Development and Reform Commission (2023C014), the Jilin University Targeted Assistance Research Funds for Rural Revitalization Project (2023-2024DDBF-0013), the Jilin Provincial Education Department Science and Technology Project (JJKH20241251KJ).
